card.title: The Doctrine of Double Effect
card.teaser: Is there a moral difference between intending harm and merely foreseeing it? A 13th-century friar says yes.
card.coverImagePrompt: A single candle flame splitting into two shadows on a stone wall, one shadow shaped like a cross, the other like a sword, medieval monastery ambiance, deep chiaroscuro

lessons.title: A medieval puzzle that still runs hospitals
lessons.body: A doctor gives a dying patient morphine to ease their pain, knowing the dose will also shorten their life. Is that murder, or mercy? For most modern ethics boards — and most courts — the answer depends on a single distinction made by Thomas Aquinas in the 1200s.

visual.imagePrompt: Soft-lit hospital room at night, a hand setting down a small glass vial beside a sleeping patient, warm muted tones

lessons.title: Intended vs. merely foreseen
lessons.body: Aquinas noticed that a single action can have two effects: one that you aim at, and another that you don't want but can foresee. The Doctrine of Double Effect says an action with a bad side effect can be permissible if four conditions hold: the act itself isn't evil, you don't intend the bad effect, the good effect isn't caused by the bad one, and there's a proportionate reason for acting.

keyTerms.definition: What you actually aim at — the goal driving the action
A consequence you know will happen but don't aim at

visual.imagePrompt: A single arrow splitting into two divergent paths, one glowing gold, one shadowed, clean symbolic diagram

lessons.title: Why the trolley problem splits people
lessons.body: Pull the lever: one person dies so five can live. Push the large man off the bridge: one dies so five can live. Outcomes identical; intuitions wildly different. Double Effect explains why. In the lever case, the death is a foreseen side effect of the redirection. In the bridge case, the man's body is the means of stopping the trolley — his death is part of what you intend. Aquinas draws the moral line exactly where most people feel it.

visual.imagePrompt: Split illustration of the two trolley dilemmas side by side, with one labeled 'foreseen' and the other 'intended', clean textbook style

lessons.title: From scholastic logic to the Geneva Conventions
lessons.body: Double Effect underlies the laws of war. Bombing an enemy munitions factory that happens to stand next to a school may be permissible; bombing the school to break enemy morale is a war crime. It also grounds medical doctrine: terminal sedation, palliative care, and even some cases of separating conjoined twins have been defended using Aquinas's four conditions, nearly a thousand years after he wrote them down.
lessons.funFact: The doctrine was codified in the Summa Theologica in a discussion about whether it's lawful to kill in self-defense — Aquinas concluded it could be, as long as death wasn't the aim.

visual.imagePrompt: Faded military treaty document beside a medical caduceus and a medieval Latin manuscript, warm archival lighting

lessons.title: The line intention draws
lessons.body: Critics argue the distinction is too slippery — you can always rephrase what you 'really' intended. Defenders argue it matches the moral structure most people already use, whether they know the Latin or not. Either way, the Doctrine of Double Effect is a rare example of a medieval argument still quietly shaping hospitals, courtrooms, and battlefields today.

quiz.question: Who originally articulated the Doctrine of Double Effect?
quiz.explanation: Aquinas introduced the doctrine in the 13th-century Summa Theologica, discussing killing in self-defense.

quiz.question: What's the key moral distinction at the heart of the doctrine?
options: Between effects you intend and effects you merely foresee
Between divine law and human law
quiz.explanation: The doctrine hinges on whether a harm is aimed at (intended) or simply anticipated as a side effect (foreseen).

quiz.question: Why does Double Effect treat pushing the man off the bridge as worse than pulling the lever?
options: Pulling the lever kills fewer people
In the bridge case, the man's death is the means — not a side effect
The lever is anonymous and therefore ethical
quiz.explanation: If the man's body is what stops the trolley, his death is part of what you intend — not a merely foreseen consequence.
